questions.q: Explain the concept of opportunity cost with suitable examples. How does it relate to the law of increasing cost?
questions.answer: Opportunity cost refers to the value of the next best alternative foregone as a result of making a decision. It is the cost of choosing one option over another. For instance, if a person chooses to buy a burger instead of a pizza, the opportunity cost is the satisfaction or pleasure they would have derived from eating the pizza. Opportunity cost is related to the law of increasing cost, which states that as the quantity of a good or service increases, the cost of producing each additional unit also increases. This is because of the law of diminishing marginal returns, where the marginal product of labor decreases as more labor is added.
marking_scheme: Define opportunity cost (1 mark)
Explain the law of increasing cost (2 marks)
Provide suitable examples (1 mark)
Relate opportunity cost to the law of increasing cost (1 mark)
questions.topic: Opportunity Cost and Law of Increasing Cost

questions.q: Suppose a firm is producing 100 units of a good at a price of ₹50 per unit. The total revenue is ₹5000. If the price increases to ₹60 per unit, what will be the new total revenue if the firm produces the same quantity of 100 units?
questions.answer:
Total Revenue (TR) = Price (P) x Quantity (Q)
TR = ₹60 x 100
TR = ₹6000
marking_scheme: Calculate the initial total revenue (1 mark)
Calculate the new price (1 mark)
Calculate the new total revenue (2 marks)
Show the correct calculation (1 mark)

questions.q: Discuss the difference between a normal good and an inferior good. Provide examples of each.
questions.answer: A normal good is a good for which the demand increases as income increases. For instance, as income increases, people tend to buy more food. An inferior good is a good for which the demand decreases as income increases. For instance, as income increases, people tend to buy less oats as they prefer more expensive and nutritious food.
marking_scheme: Define normal good (1 mark)
Define inferior good (1 mark)
Provide examples of normal good (1 mark)
Provide examples of inferior good (1 mark)
Explain the relationship between income and demand (1 mark)

questions.q: A firm is producing 50 units of a good at a price of ₹40 per unit. The total cost is ₹1500. If the price increases to ₹50 per unit, what will be the new total cost if the firm produces the same quantity of 50 units?
questions.answer:
Total Cost (TC) = Fixed Cost (FC) + Variable Cost (VC)
Since the quantity and price have increased, the fixed cost remains the same, but the variable cost increases. However, the question doesn't provide information about the fixed and variable costs. We can only assume that the total cost will increase due to the increase in price and quantity.
marking_scheme: Calculate the initial total cost (1 mark)
Calculate the new price (1 mark)
Explain the relationship between price and total cost (2 marks)
Show the correct calculation (1 mark)

questions.q: Explain the law of diminishing marginal utility. How does it relate to the marginal utility curve?
questions.answer: The law of diminishing marginal utility states that as the quantity of a good or service consumed increases, the marginal utility derived from each additional unit decreases. The marginal utility curve is a graphical representation of the law of diminishing marginal utility, where the marginal utility decreases as the quantity consumed increases.
marking_scheme: Define law of diminishing marginal utility (1 mark)
Explain the relationship between quantity and marginal utility (1 mark)
Describe the marginal utility curve (1 mark)
Relate the law to the marginal utility curve (1 mark)
Provide a suitable example (1 mark)

questions.q: A consumer is consuming 3 units of a good at a price of ₹20 per unit. The total utility is 15 utils. If the price increases to ₹30 per unit, what will be the new total utility if the consumer consumes the same quantity of 3 units?
questions.answer: Since the quantity remains the same, the total utility will decrease due to the law of diminishing marginal utility. However, the question doesn't provide information about the initial and new marginal utilities. We can only assume that the total utility will decrease due to the increase in price.
marking_scheme: Calculate the initial total utility (1 mark)
Calculate the new price (1 mark)
Explain the relationship between price and total utility (2 marks)
Show the correct calculation (1 mark)
